steps.title: Identify Maddux's core claim and how it applies to Sharif's conclusion
steps.content:
Maddux argues that there is no objective definition of productivity — reading a book can be productive or idle depending on the person. Sharif, after further research, concludes that life satisfaction drops when free time is spent “unproductively,” naming watching TV and playing games as examples. Maddux would object that labeling those activities “unproductive” assumes a universal definition he explicitly rejects.

That means the best response captures Maddux cautioning against exactly that kind of broad assumption.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice
steps.content:
• "By acknowledging that free time is more likely to enhance life satisfaction when it is spent productively than when it is spent unproductively": Maddux does not endorse Sharif’s framework; he challenges whether “productive” is even a coherent universal category. ✗
  • "By challenging the reasoning in Text 1, as it has not been proved that productivity commonly contributes to individuals' life satisfaction": Maddux’s objection is not that productivity doesn’t matter, but that what counts as productive is subjective. This misstates his argument. ✗
  • "By warning against making an overly broad assumption, as there is no clear consensus in distinguishing between productive and unproductive activities": This directly reflects Maddux’s stated position that “unproductive” is not an objective category, so applying it to TV and games assumes a consensus that doesn’t exist. ✓
  • "By claiming that the specific activities named in Text 1 are actually examples of productive activities rather than unproductive ones": Maddux wouldn’t declare TV and games “productive” — his point is that the distinction is subjective, not that these activities fall on one side of it. ✗
That matches the correct answer — C is correct.
